request.question: Were both Elizabeth Chadwick and Connie May Fowler authors?

title: Nancy Fairbanks
Elizabeth Chadwick
Beale family
Alan Chadwick
Connie May Fowler
Stephen F. Chadwick
Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon
Before Women Had Wings
Fowler's position
Owen Chadwick
sentences: Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.
Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.
She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.
The Beales, together with the Fowlers, are a fictional family in the BBC soap opera "EastEnders".
They were the main family for storylines in early "EastEnders" and remained as such for many years.
Having appeared continuously from episode one, they are the show's longest serving family.
In 2004 Peter Beale and Lucy Beale were recast to give them a more mature look.
In 2007, there were few members of the Beale family left and no Fowlers, so the family were revamped.
Peter was recast again.
Ian Beale started a relationship with Jane Beale and Steven Beale also returned.
By 2010, the number of Beales had fallen again.
Lucy was recast and in 2013, Peter and Bobby were recast and Cindy Williams Jnr, the half-sister of Peter and Lucy, was reintroduced.
In addition to this at the start of 2014 Bex Fowler returned to the serial with her mother Sonia Fowler, with Martin Fowler following 11 months later in December.
In 2014 Lucy is found dead, causing her family and friends to struggle to come to terms with her death and the secrets she left behind, putting many people in the frame for her murder.
It was revealed on 19 February 2015 that she was killed by her own little brother, Bobby.
It was also revealed that Kathy Beale, who was presumed dead in 2006, was alive after having faked her death with her new husband in South Africa.
In December 2016 Michelle Fowler returned to Albert Square after originally leaving in 1995, although now played by Jenna Russell.
Members of the Beale family by blood currently in "EastEnders" include Ian, Michelle, Martin, Bex and Louie.
Additionally, several others may be considered Beales through marriage; Jane, Kathy, Sonia and Stacey Fowler are among these.
Also, Whitney Dean was adopted by Bianca Butcher, who is a member of the Beale family.
Lily Fowler and Arthur Fowler have both taken Martin's surname and have him as their stepfather.

Alan Chadwick (July 27, 1909 – May 25, 1980), English master gardener, was a leading innovator of organic farming techniques and influential educator in the field of biodynamic/French intensive gardening.
He was a student of Rudolf Steiner and is often cited as inspirational to the development of the "California Cuisine" movement.
The Chadwick restaurant in Beverly Hills was named after him.
His grave is marked by a stupa at the Green Gulch Farm Zen Center in California.

Chadwick is the subject of a 2013 retrospective by a former University of California, Santa Cruz professor, Paul Lee, called "There Is a Garden in the Mind: A Memoir of Alan Chadwick and the Organic Movement in California".
There is a memorial page at http://www.alan-chadwick.org/ that includes the 1971 16-mm film "Garden".
Connie May Fowler (born January 3, 1960 to parents of multi-cultural backgrounds) is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet.
Her semi-autobiographical novel, "Before Women had Wings", received the 1996 Southern Book Critics Circle Award and the Francis Buck Award (League of American Pen Women).
She adapted the novel for Oprah Winfrey and the subsequent Emmy-winning film starred Winfrey, Ellen Barkin, Julia Stiles, and Tina Majorino.
"Remembering Blue" received the Chautauqua South Literary Award.
Three of her novels were Dublin International Literary Award nominees.
Her other novels include "Sugar Cage" and "River of Hidden Dreams".
"The Problem with Murmur Lee" was Redbook’s premier book club selection.
Her memoir, "When Katie Wakes", explores her family’s generational cycle of domestic violence.
"How Clarissa Burden Learned to Fly", a novel oft compared to Virginia Woolf's "Mrs. Dalloway" in term of its structure, was published in 2010.
Her latest book, a memoir titled "A Million Fragile bones," will be published April 20, 2017 by Twisted Road Publications.
It explores her life on an isolated barrier island and the horrific impact and aftermath of the Deepwater Horizon BP oil spill.
Her books have been translated into eighteen languages (http://www.conniemayfowler.com/about.html).
Stephen Fowler Chadwick (December 25, 1825 – January 15, 1895) was an American Democratic politician who served as the fifth Governor of Oregon from 1877 to 1878.
Governor Chadwick was the first person to obtain the governorship by way of the state's Line of Succession.
Theophilus Hastings, 7th Earl of Huntingdon PC (10 December 1650 – 30 May 1701) was an English politician.
He was the son of Ferdinando Hastings, 6th Earl of Huntingdon, born in the 27th year of his parents' marriage, and became Earl of Huntingdon on 13 February 1656 on his father's death.
He married, firstly, Elizabeth Lewis, daughter of Sir John Lewis, 1st Baronet, on 19 February 1671/2.
She died 24th December 1688.
He married, secondly, Mary Frances Fowler, daughter of Francis Leveson Fowler, on 8 May 1690.
With his first wife he had two sons and six daughters, including Thomas (1674–1675), George and Elizabeth.
With his second wife he had two more sons and five more daughters, including Theophilus, Catherine, Maria and Margaret.
Before Women Had Wings is a 1997 television film, based on the story by Connie May Fowler about a mother whose abusive husband commits suicide.
She then starts to violently abuse her two daughters.
It is only then that a kindly black woman becomes good friends with her youngest daughter and helps the daughter escape her tortured life.
The film stars Ellen Barkin, Oprah Winfrey, Tina Majorino, and Julia Stiles.
Barkin won an Emmy for her role.
In medicine, Fowler's position is a standard patient position.
It is an intervention used to promote oxygenation via maximum chest expansion and is implemented during events of respiratory distress.
Fowler's position facilitates the relaxing of tension of the abdominal muscles, allowing for improved breathing.
In immobile patients and infants, the Fowler's position alleviates compression of the chest that occurs due to gravity.
Fowler's position increases comfort during eating and other activities, is used in postpartum women to improve uterine drainage, and in infants when signs of respiratory distress present.
The patient is placed in a semi-upright sitting position (45-60 degrees) and may have knees either bent or straight.
Fowler's position is also used when oral or nasal gastric feeding tubes have been implemented as it minimizes the risk of aspiration.
Peristalsis and swallowing are aided by the effect of gravitational pull.
William Owen Chadwick ( ; 20 May 1916 – 17 July 2015) was a British Anglican priest, academic, writer and prominent historian of Christianity.
As a leading academic, Chadwick became Dixie Professor of Ecclesiastical History from 1958 to 1968 and Regius Professor of History from 1968 to 1983.
From 1956 to 1983, Chadwick was elected and severed as the Master of Selwyn College, Cambridge.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

He was a student of Rudolf Steiner and is often cited as inspirational to the development of the "California Cuisine" movement.

Stephen F. Chadwick | Stephen Fowler Chadwick (December 25, 1825 – January 15, 1895) was an American Democratic politician who served as the fifth Governor of Oregon from 1877 to 1878.  Governor Chadwick was the first person to obtain the governorship by way of the state's Line of Succession.
Lilian Fowler | Elizabeth Lilian Maud Fowler MBE, JP (7 June 1886 – 11 May 1954) was an Australian politician, and Australia's first female mayor.

Nancy Fairbanks | Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.

Connie May Fowler | Connie May Fowler (born January 3, 1960 to parents of multi-cultural backgrounds) is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet.  Her semi-autobiographical novel, "Before Women had Wings", received the 1996 Southern Book Critics Circle Award and the Francis Buck Award (League of American Pen Women).  She adapted the novel for Oprah Winfrey and the subsequent Emmy-winning film starred Winfrey, Ellen Barkin, Julia Stiles, and Tina Majorino.  "Remembering Blue" received the Chautauqua South Literary Award.  Three of her novels were Dublin International Literary Award nominees.  Her other novels include "Sugar Cage" and "River of Hidden Dreams".  "The Problem with Murmur Lee" was Redbook’s premier book club selection.  Her memoir, "When Katie Wakes", explores her family’s generational cycle of domestic violence.  "How Clarissa Burden Learned to Fly", a novel oft compared to Virginia Woolf's "Mrs. Dalloway" in term of its structure, was published in 2010.  Her latest book, a memoir titled "A Million Fragile bones," will be published April 20, 2017 by Twisted Road Publications.  It explores her life on an isolated barrier island and the horrific impact and aftermath of the Deepwater Horizon BP oil spill.  Her books have been translated into eighteen languages (http://www.conniemayfowler.com/about.html).

Connie Needham | Connie Needham (born December 5, 1959) is an American actress and dance instructor.  As Connie Newton, she portrayed Elizabeth Bradford on the television series "Eight is Enough" from 1977 to 1981.
Conny Van Dyke | Conny Van Dyke (sometimes credited as Connie Van Dyke) is a singer and actress, born September 28, 1945 in Nassawadox, Virginia to Benjamin and Charlotte Elizabeth Van Dyke.
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: the name "Elizabeth Chadwick" (possible real name or pseudonym) and Connie May Fowler (author).

- From passage [1]: Elizabeth Chadwick "is an author of historical fictions."
- From passage [4]: Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is "the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick."
- From passage [5]: Connie May Fowler "is an American novelist, essayist, memoirist, screenwriter, and poet." (born "January 3, 1960").

Ambiguity / missing fact:
- Ambiguous entity: "Elizabeth Chadwick" — could be (A) an independent author (passage [1]) or (B) a pseudonym used by Nancy Fairbanks Herndon (passage [4]). It is not resolved here whether these refer to the same person or two distinct authors. Missing fact (bridge): definitive identity and disambiguation of "Elizabeth Chadwick" (real person details or confirmation that it is Nancy Fairbanks' pseudonym).

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1) Fetch page "Elizabeth Chadwick" (article lede/infobox and "Career" or "Works") — confirm birth, nationality, and that she "is an author of historical fictions" and whether she is a distinct author.
2) Fetch "Nancy Fairbanks" / "Nancy Fairbanks Herndon" (lede and "Pseudonyms" or "Works") — verify the quoted phrase "under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick" and dates/works tied to that pseudonym.
3) Fetch "Connie May Fowler" (lede/infobox) to confirm author credits and published works (already supported by passage [5]).
4) If needed, search: "Elizabeth Chadwick pseudonym Nancy Fairbanks" or "Elizabeth Chadwick disambiguation" to find disambiguation pages or redirects.
hop2_docs: Nancy Fairbanks | Nancy Fairbanks Herndon is a US lecturer in English and the author of historical romances under the pseudonym Elizabeth Chadwick and mysteries under the name Nancy Herndon and Nancy Fairbanks.
Elizabeth Chadwick | Elizabeth Chadwick is an author of historical fictions.  She is a member of Regia Anglorum, a Medieval reenactment organisation.

Elizabeth Neff Walker | Elizabeth Rotter is an American author of romance novels.  She has been published under the pseudonyms Elizabeth Walker, Elizabeth Neff Walker, and Laura Matthews.  As Laura Matthews, she has released more than 30 Regency romance novels.  Under her other pseudonyms, she writes mainstream women's fiction or contemporary romances, most of them revolving around people working at a hospital.

Paul Chadwick (author) | Paul Chadwick (1902–1972) was a pulp magazine author who wrote many stories under his own name and various pseudonyms.  As was the case with many prolific contributors to the pulps, he wrote in a number of different genres including detective stories, science fiction and westerns.  He created "Secret Agent X", published under the "house name" of Brant House, and also wrote the one and only issue of the "Doc Savage" clone "Captain Hazzard" (May 1938) under the name of Chester Hawks.

Elizabeth Kata | Elizabeth Colina Katayama (née McDonald ; 9 October 19124 September 1998) was an Australian writer under the pseudonym Elizabeth Kata, best known for "Be Ready with Bells and Drums" (1961), which was made into the award-winning film "A Patch of Blue" (1965).
Mary Elizabeth Hawker | Mary Elizabeth Hawker (1848–1908) was an English writer of novellas and short stories.  From 1890 onwards she wrote under the pseudonym Lanoe Falconer.

Chadwick Professor of Civil Engineering | The Chadwick Professor of Civil Engineering is a professorship at University College London.  It is named after Sir Edwin Chadwick, a Victorian social reformer who worked to improve sanitation conditions.  The professorship was established in 1898 as the Chadwick Professorship of Municipal Engineering, with Edwin Chadwick's son Osbert Chadwick serving as the first professor.
response.summary_2: Yes; both Elizabeth Chadwick and Connie May Fowler are authors. Elizabeth Chadwick is described as an author of historical fiction and a member of the medieval reenactment group Regia Anglorum. Connie May Fowler is an American novelist, essayist and memoirist (born January 3, 1960).
